---
title: "About"
---

I came to data science slowly, and mostly by accident. I studied psychology at UC Santa Barbara, and while I was fascinated by how people make decisions, I left school without much of a quantitative foundation. A math post-bacc at CUNY a few years later helped me start to close that gap, and I've been filling it in ever since — mostly on the job, learning from people who were further along than me. Whatever I know about using data in a business I picked up one role at a time, and I still feel like I'm learning the craft.

## Starting in Support

My first real exposure to business data was in support at ZocDoc. I wasn't doing analytics — I was answering tickets. But the pattern you notice after a few thousand conversations with users is that the same issues come up in clusters, and nobody upstream always knows which ones. I started pulling ticket data into spreadsheets and surfacing the patterns to the product team. It wasn't modeling; it was showing up with a clear picture of what customers were running into. That turned out to be the most useful thing I did there, and it set the template for the work I've been doing ever since.

## Product Specialist at Square

I joined Square as a product specialist during the hardware transition from swipe to chip to contactless readers. The job was effectively bridging support and product — sitting with the CS org, understanding where customers were getting stuck, and translating that into signals the product team could act on. Over five years I grew into an analytics role. The work I'm most proud of from that time was building a classifier that automated a chunk of support case tagging, which freed up a meaningful amount of CS capacity. But the bigger thing Square taught me was how to operate at the seam between support operations and product roadmap — where the data is messy, the stakeholders have different goals, and the value is in making sure the right evidence gets to the right decision.

I also taught a data analytics course at General Assembly in 2017 while I was at Square — 10 weeks of SQL, statistics, and experimentation with career changers. Teaching a room full of people who are seeing this stuff for the first time is the fastest way to find out whether you actually understand it yourself.

## Building DS Chops at Atlassian

I joined Atlassian in 2020 as the first data scientist hire for the events team. Events was a good first DS role because the scope was contained enough that I could build the pipeline end-to-end — instrumentation, measurement, experimentation — without inheriting someone else's stack. From there I moved into marketing data science, where the work scaled up considerably: Ideal Customer Profile modeling, buying group analysis, propensity scoring for the enterprise GTM org. I got to apply what I'd learned about operating at the seam of multiple functions, but now with real modeling tools behind it.

Toward the end of my time there I spent a lot of energy on how LLMs could change the shape of DS work itself. The honest take is that they're already a meaningful unlock — they compress the boring parts of the loop (querying, scaffolding, summarizing) so more time goes toward the judgment calls that actually drive recommendations. But the unlock only holds if you pair it with the verification that today's models still require: checking the join, sanity-checking the numbers, pressure-testing the framing before it goes to a stakeholder. The teams that get this right treat the LLM as a force multiplier; the ones that don't ship plausible-looking work that's quietly wrong.

A lot of what I ended up doing at Atlassian was helping the rest of the data org get to the first category — setting up MCP connections, writing knowledge files, sitting with people through their first few workflows, and being honest about what still needed a human in the loop. That work turned into the adoption playbook I've written up separately, and it's easily the part of the last few years I've enjoyed most. It pulled on the same thing I liked about teaching at General Assembly — watching someone go from "I don't know how this works" to using it fluently is the best feedback loop there is.

## Where I Am Now

I was laid off from Atlassian in March 2026 as part of a broader restructure. I'm based in San Francisco and looking for senior data science roles — ideally ones where the work is close to the business, where I can use the progression from support to product to DS as an advantage rather than something to explain away, and where I can keep figuring out how AI tooling actually changes the shape of this kind of work.

## Education

- **B.A. Psychology** — UC Santa Barbara
- **Post-Baccalaureate, Mathematics** — City University of New York
